metadata.working_title: A translational MRI approach to validate acute axonal damage detection

abstract_sketch.problem: Axonal degeneration is a core pathological feature of neurodegenerative disease and is associated with irreversible clinical disability, yet noninvasive in vivo detection of axonal damage remains limited in specificity and has rarely been validated.
abstract_sketch.gap: Conventional diffusion MRI cannot disentangle axons from myelin; prior AxCaliber applications were restricted to single-fiber-orientation voxels (e.g., corpus callosum) whereas >=70% of brain voxels contain multiple fiber orientations; and histological validation of imaging biomarkers of axonal damage is rarely performed.
abstract_sketch.approach: We use a modified multicompartment AxCaliber framework that accounts for crossing fibers to map whole-brain axonal diameter. We validate it in a rat model of acute axonal damage (unilateral stereotaxic injection of ibotenic acid into the dorsal hippocampus, damaging the fimbria) against neurofilament/MBP/NeuN histology, then deploy the matched protocol on a 3T Connectom scanner in 10 relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis (MS) patients and 6 age-matched controls.
abstract_sketch.key_results: In rats (n=9), the injected fimbria showed a significant increase in mean estimated axonal diameter (p=0.003), with ANOVA revealing main effects of injection (F1,7=19.5, p=0.003), tract position (F98,686=219.5, p<0.001) and their interaction (F98,686=11.2, p=0.012). Neurofilament intensity correlated with MRI-derived axonal diameter (r=0.54, p=0.029). In MS patients, whole-brain TBSS revealed diffusely increased axonal diameter in NAWM, and a significant group*lesion-type interaction (F1,235=40.1, p=0.03; group effect F1,235=25.7, p<0.001) localized the effect to T1-hypointense lesions.
abstract_sketch.impact: Axonal diameter mapping is a sensitive and histologically validated noninvasive imaging biomarker of axonal damage, enabling patient stratification in MS and monitoring of axonal degeneration across neurological disease.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Axonal damage underlies irreversible disability in MS and other neurodegenerative diseases; transition to progressive MS occurs when an axonal-loss threshold is reached.
Diffusion MRI is sensitive to white-matter microstructure but is nonspecific to axons vs. myelin, and validation against ex vivo data is rarely performed.
AxCaliber estimates axonal diameter but in its original form requires single fiber orientation; >=70% of brain voxels contain >=2 fiber orientations, so whole-brain mapping has been out of reach.
Prior work reported higher axonal diameter in the NAWM corpus callosum of MS patients but was restricted to one tract and lacked histological validation.
We introduce a translational pipeline: a modified crossing-fiber AxCaliber model validated against an ibotenic-acid rat fimbria-damage model and then applied whole-brain in MS.

Specific objectives: (i) validate AxCaliber against histology in rats; (iia) compare whole-brain voxelwise axonal diameter between MS patients and controls; (iib) compare lesion vs contralateral NAWM vs homologous healthy-control WM; (iic) stratify lesions by T1 intensity (isointense vs hypointense).

subsections.title: A rat model of acute axonal damage
content_bullets: 14 days after unilateral ibotenic-acid injection (2.5 ug/ul, 1 ul, bregma -3.8 mm, sup-inf 3.0 mm, 2 mm from midline) in n=9 rats, DTI-based tractography of the fimbria showed significantly increased mean axonal diameter in the injected hemisphere (paired t, p=0.003).

Repeated-measures ANOVA across the antero-posterior axis of the tract (100 steps): injection F1,7=19.5, p=0.003; position F98,686=219.5, p<0.001; injection x position F98,686=11.2, p=0.012; differences were localized posterior to the injection site.

subsections.title: Immunohistochemical validation
content_bullets: NeuN staining intensity in hippocampus decreased on the injected side (paired t, p=0.026).
Neurofilament staining intensity in fimbria increased on the injected side (paired t, p=0.047).
MBP (myelin) staining showed no significant hemispheric difference, indicating axonal changes precede myelin loss at 14 days (Fig. S1).
Neurofilament intensity correlated with MRI-derived axonal diameter across hemispheres (r=0.54, p=0.029).
One of nine animals showed no hippocampal damage and was excluded.

subsections.title: Axonal damage in MS normal-appearing white matter
content_bullets: 10 relapsing-remitting MS patients (7F, age 27-59) and 6 controls (3F, age 23-53); age not different (Mann-Whitney U, p=0.093).

TBSS voxelwise comparison: significantly increased axonal diameter in MS NAWM vs controls (p<0.05, corrected), symmetric across hemispheres and involving corpus callosum, internal capsule, corona radiata, thalamic radiation, inferior longitudinal fasciculus, cingulum, fornix, superior longitudinal fasciculus, inferior fronto-occipital fasciculus, uncinate fasciculus, and tapetum.
The reverse contrast (controls>MS) was not significant.

subsections.title: Axonal pathology in MS lesions
content_bullets: Normalized index of asymmetry (NIA): lesions vs contralateral NAWM in MS patients differed from zero (one-sample t, p<0.001).
General linear model across lesion type (iso vs hypointense on T1) and cohort (MS vs healthy ROI): group effect F1,235=25.7, p<0.001; group*lesion-type interaction F1,235=40.1, p=0.03.
Post hoc: axonal diameter was significantly increased only in T1-hypointense lesions (p<0.01), not in isointense lesions.

subsections.title: Clinical associations
content_bullets: No significant association between axonal diameter and EDSS or SDMT in lesions or NAWM.

Trend-level negative association between axonal diameter and disease duration in whole WM skeleton (TBSS); ROI-based analyses reached significance in left cingulum and right inferior fronto-occipital fasciculus (Fig. S2).

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Preclinical validation: increased neurofilament staining without MBP change shows that AxCaliber-measured axonal-diameter increase reflects axonal morphological alteration (varicosities/spheroids) before myelin loss.
The significant neurofilament-diameter correlation (r=0.54, p=0.029) validates the imaging marker as a proxy for axonal damage, complementing prior electron-microscopy comparisons in healthy tissue.

Clinical translation: crossing-fiber AxCaliber enables whole-brain axonal-diameter mapping, revealing diffuse NAWM pathology across all major tracts-extending beyond the corpus-callosum-only findings of prior work.
Lesion stratification: selective increase in T1-hypointense lesions aligns with the known greater axonal damage in hypointense vs isointense lesions and suggests an imaging-based severity stratifier.
Lack of EDSS/SDMT correlation likely reflects the modest early-stage cohort and compensatory mechanisms.

Limitations: neurofilament staining (not electron microscopy) is used for validation; multicompartment models make fixed-diffusivity and no-exchange assumptions; only the fimbria was validated; MRI contributions from glia/oligodendrocytes cannot be fully excluded.
Outlook: axonal-diameter mapping as a biomarker for early detection, lesion stratification and treatment monitoring in MS and broader neurodegeneration.

subsections.title: Animal model and stereotaxic injections
content_bullets: n=9 rats; unilateral injection of 1 ul ibotenic acid at 2.5 ug/ul in right dorsal hippocampus (bregma -3.8 mm, sup-inf 3.0 mm, 2 mm from midline); contralateral saline control.
MRI and perfusion 14 days post-injection.
Ethics: Instituto de Neurociencias de Alicante IACUC; Spanish law 32/2007; EU directive 86/609, decree 2001-486, recommendation 2007/526/EC; Project MRI-STRUCTURE 677/2018; ARRIVE 10 checklist.

subsections.title: MRI acquisition
content_bullets: Rats: 7T Bruker BioSpect 70/30; EPI stimulated-echo DW, 31 directions, b=0(1)/2000(15)/4000(15) s/mm^2; Delta=15/25/40/60 ms; TR=5000/TE=25 ms; 124 volumes; 14 slices, FOV 25x25 mm^2, matrix 110x110, 0.225x0.225 mm in-plane, 0.6 mm slice; 36 min.

Humans: Siemens 3T Connectom (300 mT/m, 64-ch array); EPI DW, 91 directions, b=0(1)/2000(30)/4000(60) s/mm^2; Delta=17/35/61 ms; TR=5000/TE=89 ms; 273 volumes; 82 slices, FOV 220x220 mm^2, matrix 110x110, 2x2 mm in-plane, 2 mm slice; 24 min; plus 1 mm isotropic T1 MEMPRAGE and FLAIR.

subsections.title: Tissue processing and immunohistochemistry
content_bullets: Perfusion with 100 ml PBS + 100 ml 4% PFA; 1 h post-fix; 3% agarose/PBS; 50 um vibratome coronal sections.

Primary antibodies (all 1:250): MBP (Millipore MAB384-1ML, AB_240837), NF-M 160 kD (Abcam ab134458, AB_2860025), NeuN (Millipore MAB377, AB_2298772); secondaries 1:500 (Molecular Probes A-11029 AB_2534088; A-11042 AB_2534099); DAPI 15 mM; antigen retrieval for myelin (1% citrate + 0.05% Tween 20, 80 degrees C).
Imaging on Leica DM4000 with Neurolucida; fluorescence quantified with Icy; NeuN ROIs 200 um^2 (>=5 slices/rat); MBP/NF ROIs 400 um^2.

subsections.title: Model fitting and statistical analysis
content_bullets: AxCaliber modified for fiber dispersion (multi-orientation) fit in MATLAB R2015b.
Rat DTI tractography of fimbria in ExploreDTI at lowest Delta/b; paired t test across hemispheres; RM-ANOVA with correction for multiple comparisons along tract.
Human data preprocessed in FreeSurfer 5.3.0 / FSL 5.0 (gradient nonlinearity, motion, eddy, b-matrix rotation).
Lesion segmentation on FLAIR (3D-Slicer 4.2.0); contralateral NAWM via coregistration; T1 hypo/isointense classification.
TBSS variant with ANTs coregistration (Klein 2009) and lesion-masked permutation GLM (Winkler 2014), TFCE, age as covariate.
Lesion vs healthy-cohort comparison: GLM with lesion-type (iso/hypo) between factor and cohort (healthy/MS) within factor, group*lesion-type interaction, post hoc t tests, Bonferroni correction.
NIA (normalized index of asymmetry) for hemispheric comparisons.
